modules.title: Why a routine — not products
modules.html:
Why a routine — not products

"I had a shelf full of bottles and hair that stayed dry. The day I understood the problem wasn't in the products, everything changed." The first mistake most of us make is searching for the miracle product. We buy, we test, we abandon. Social media feeds this spiral: every month, a new oil, a new serum, a new technique presented as revolutionary. And yet the hair stays dry.

A product cannot replace a method

The truth is simpler — and more liberating: Type 4 hair isn't difficult. It has precise needs that generic advice ignores. What it needs isn't a better product. It's a consistent sequence, repeated in the right order, adapted to your specific hair structure.

A routine is exactly that: a sequence. Water first. Always. Then nourishment. Then sealing. Then protection. In that order. Every wash day. Not because you were told to — because you understand why that order works.

What you'll build here

This guide takes you from zero to a complete, personalized, lasting routine. You'll learn to diagnose your hair with precision, understand the chemistry of the ingredients you use, organize your week into a rhythm, adjust for the seasons, and solve any problem that comes up — without panic, without buying new products.

There's no brand list here. No mandatory purchases. A system you can apply with what you already have — and intelligently improve over time.

SCHICGIRL TIP — BEFORE YOU CONTINUE

Take a photo of your hair today — even unstyled, even if you don't love it. In 90 days, this photo will be your proof that consistency transforms what products alone cannot.

KEY TAKEAWAY

A product moisturizes for a day. A routine moisturizes for years. Consistency is the active ingredient nobody sells — and this guide helps you cultivate it.

INTRODUCTION

modules.title: The four pillars of a routine that lasts
modules.html:
The four pillars of a routine that lasts

"I tried dozens of routines. The ones that held all had these four things in common." A lasting hair routine doesn't depend on a high budget or a large product collection. It rests on four pillars, each irreplaceable. Miss one, and the routine holds for two weeks before collapsing.

Pillar 1

SELF-KNOWLEDGE

Hair pattern, porosity, distress signals.

Without this precise diagnosis, everything that follows is guesswork. This guide starts here — intentionally. Knowing your profile turns every product decision into an informed choice.

Pillar 2

THE SEQUENCE

Water first. Always. Then care products in the right order for your porosity. Then sealing.

Then protection. Changing the order changes the result. Applying the same products in the wrong order is often the cause of hair that "doesn't respond." Pillar 3

CONSISTENCY

A weekly deep condition. Nightly protection. A mid-week refresh, 2 to 3 times. This simple rhythm, held for 90 days, produces results that no product in the world can replicate.

Pillar 4

ADAPTABILITY

Hair changes. Seasons change. Life changes.

The routines that last are the ones you know how to adjust — without starting over. This guide gives you the tools to read those signals and respond intelligently.

THE SCHICGIRL METHOD IN FOUR WORDS

Saturate · Nourish · Seal · Protect. This sequence is the backbone of this entire guide. Once memorized, it becomes a reflex. When you feel lost, come back to these four words.

PART ONE

modules.title: Hair anatomy — what a strand is really made of
modules.html:
Hair anatomy — what a strand is really made of

"Once I understood that my hair was built in layers — and which layer my dryness lived in — every product label finally made sense." You don't need a science degree to care for your hair beautifully. But understanding what a single strand is actually made of changes everything: it turns guessing into knowing. Every strand on your head is built like a tiny cable in three layers — and almost every problem you've ever had with moisture, breakage, or shine traces back to one of them.

A SINGLE TYPE 4 STRAND · OUTER LAYER TO CORE

Cuticle

OUTER LAYER

Overlapping scales like roof tiles. Your hair's gatekeeper — it decides how easily water enters and how fast it escapes. This is what porosity measures.

Cortex

MIDDLE LAYER

The thickest layer and the heart of your strand. Holds the protein (keratin)

that gives strength and elasticity, the bonds that create your coil shape, and most of your hair's water.

Medulla

INNER CORE

A soft, sometimes hollow center. Often absent in fine hair. It plays the smallest day-to-day role in how your hair feels.

FIGURE 1 — THE THREE LAYERS OF A HAIR STRAND

The cuticle — your hair's roof

The cuticle is the outermost layer, made of transparent, overlapping scales. When those scales lie flat and closed, your hair retains moisture, reflects light, and feels smooth. When they're raised or chipped, water rushes in but evaporates just as fast, and strands catch on each other and break. Heat, harsh shampoos, color, and rough handling raise the cuticle; cool water, slightly acidic rinses, gentle proteins, and sealing oils help it lie flat again. Porosity is simply the state of your cuticle — which is why it's the most important thing you'll diagnose in this book.

The cortex — where strength and curl actually live

Beneath the cuticle sits the cortex — up to 90% of the strand. This is where your keratin protein lives (strength and elasticity), where the internal bonds that create your coil pattern are held, and where most of your water is stored. When people say hair needs "protein and moisture balance," they're really talking about the cortex. Too little moisture here and strands snap; too little protein and they stretch like chewing gum and never bounce back. The cortex is the layer your deep conditioners are trying to reach — and the cuticle is the door they have to get through first.

The medulla — the quiet core

The innermost medulla is a soft, air-filled core that's often missing entirely in fine strands. For Type 4 hair care, it's the layer you'll think about least — but it's why coarse strands feel so different from fine ones, a difference you'll learn to use in the thickness chapter.

What happens when Type 4 hair loses moisture

Here's the chain reaction that explains your dry days. Type 4 coils make it hard for your scalp's natural oils to travel down the strand, so your cuticle is more exposed than straighter hair. When moisture leaves the cortex, the strand loses its flexibility — it goes from a spring that bends to a twig that snaps. A dehydrated cuticle also lifts slightly, so strands tangle and grab at each other. Every coil and every angle becomes a weak point. This is why dryness and breakage are the same problem wearing two faces — and why your whole routine is built to get water into the cortex and keep it there.

SCHICGIRL TIP — PICTURE THE DOOR BEFORE THE ROOM

Whenever a product "won't absorb," picture the cuticle: the door is shut (low porosity) and the product is sitting on the porch. A little warmth opens it. Whenever your hair drinks water but is dry by night, picture a door that won't stay closed (high porosity) — your job is sealing, not more water.

COMMON MISTAKE

Trying to "feed protein" to hair that only needs water. Protein lives in the cortex, but you can't push it in through a parched, raised cuticle without first restoring moisture — and stacking protein on dry hair is the fastest route to the stiff, straw-like snapping we'll fix in the balance chapter.

KEY POINT

Three layers, one rule: protect the cuticle, hydrate the cortex, and the medulla takes care of itself. Every technique in this guide is one of those two jobs in disguise.

modules.title: The science of Type 4 hair
modules.html:
The science of Type 4 hair

"I was stacking products without knowing why. Understanding the science let me do less — and get far more."

The spiral structure — a built-in challenge

Type 4 hair grows in tight spirals, sometimes at near-right angles. The scalp's natural sebum — the lubricant produced by hair follicles — glides easily down a straight strand, but struggles to travel each tight coil of Type 4 hair. Result: your ends, the oldest and most exposed part of your hair, are chronically drier than your roots.

Why your ends are always thirstiest

On straighter hair, sebum slides from scalp to tip in a day. On Type 4 hair it stalls at the first bend — which means your ends, the oldest and most weathered part of your strand, almost never receive natural oil. They've also survived months of washing, styling, and friction, so their cuticle is the most raised. The lesson lands early: your ends need the most moisture and the most sealing, every single time, while your roots usually need the least.

Humectants, emollients, occlusives — the trinity

Three ingredient families work together, in this precise order:

HUMECTANTS

Attract and hold water in the fiber. Glycerin, aloe vera, raw honey, panthenol. Look for them near the top of the label.

EMOLLIENTS

Soften and smooth the cuticle. Shea butter, avocado oil, jojoba, mango butter. They nourish the fiber.

OCCLUSIVES

Form a barrier that slows evaporation. Castor oil, petrolatum, lanolin.

Always your last layer.

The elasticity test — your baseline diagnostic

Gently stretch a wet strand. Stretches and returns = good protein/moisture balance. Snaps cleanly = needs moisture. Stretches without returning = needs protein. Stiff, hard, won't stretch = protein overload. This 5-second test is your most reliable weekly diagnostic.

KEY POINT

Hydration is a system, not a product. Water + retention + protection = supple hair for days. Understanding all three lets you solve almost any problem.

modules.title: Identifying your hair pattern
modules.html:
Identifying your hair pattern

"For years I followed routines designed for someone else's hair. The day I learned to read my own pattern, everything I'd read finally made sense." Your hair pattern — 4A, 4B, or 4C — describes the natural shape of your coils on wet, product- free hair. It influences how sebum travels along the strand, the surface area exposed to dry air, and the styling methods that will work best for you. It's not a hierarchy. It's a valuable map.

TYPE 4A

Soft S-Coils · Defined · Visible Springiness Shrinkage 40–60%

WHAT IT LOVES

Light, water-rich layers. Defined wash-and-go styles. Frequent re-moisturizing — your coils reform beautifully with water.

ITS BIGGEST CHALLENGE

Heavy butters crush definition. And because it seems "easy," deep conditioning gets skipped — a costly mistake for length retention.

IDEAL METHOD

LCO if low porosity, LOC if high. Stay lighter than your Type 4 sisters in terms of product richness.

WEEKLY RHYTHM

Co-wash or gentle cleanse, weekly deep condition, water-based re-moisturizing every 2–3 days.

TYPE 4B

Sharp Z-Pattern · Fluffy Volume · Defined Angles Shrinkage 60–70%

WHAT IT LOVES

Creamy leave-ins, the LOC method, twist-outs and braid-outs that highlight elongation. Slip during detangling is essential.

ITS BIGGEST CHALLENGE

The angles are exactly where moisture escapes and breakage begins. Every angle is a vulnerability point to protect.

IDEAL METHOD

LOC for most. Finger-detangle on slip. Seal ends at every application, no exceptions.

WEEKLY RHYTHM

Cleanse, weekly moisturizing deep condition, mid-week greenhouse refresh to boost retention.

TYPE 4C

Tightly Coiled · Maximum Shrinkage · Dense and Majestic Shrinkage 70–80%

WHAT IT LOVES

Generous water, rich creams, thick sealing oils on the ends, and low-manipulation styles that leave it alone.

ITS BIGGEST CHALLENGE

Sebum simply cannot make the journey from root to tip. Every coil is an obstacle — sealing the ends is non-negotiable.

IDEAL METHOD

LOC with a thick cream last. The greenhouse method 1–2× a week is transformative for this profile.

WEEKLY RHYTHM

Gentle cleanse, weekly deep condition with heat, daily end-sealing, well-moisturized protective styles.

SCHICGIRL TIP — THE REALITY OF MIXED PATTERNS

Most women are a mix: a 4B crown with 4C edges, or 4A lengths that become 4C at the nape. Divide your hair into 4 sections and note the pattern of each. Build your routine around the dominant pattern, then treat drier zones (often the nape and edges) with extra richness and care.

modules.title: Understanding and testing your porosity
modules.html:
Understanding and testing your porosity

"When I discovered my porosity, I stopped chasing every new trend. I finally knew what my hair actually needed." Porosity is the single most decisive factor in your product and method choices. It describes how easily your cuticles let water in and out. It decides whether your LOC should flip to LCO, whether you need heat during your deep condition, and why a product that works for your friend doesn't work for you.

Low Porosity

TIGHTLY CLOSED CUTICLES

Water enters slowly — products often sit on the surface. But once moisture gets in, it stays a long time. Hair dries slowly. Buildup is your main enemy.

Your method: LCO. Gentle heat during deep conditioning. Light, humectant-rich products.

Clarify regularly to avoid buildup.

Medium Porosity

BALANCED CUTICLES

Water enters and exits at an even pace. The easiest to maintain. Most standard routines work well. Maintain consistency and avoid over-manipulation.

Your method: LOC or LCO based on feel.

Weekly deep condition. Adjust for the season.

High Porosity

RAISED OR GAPPED CUTICLES

Water enters almost instantly — and leaves just as fast. Often caused or worsened by color, direct heat, chemical treatments, and rough handling. Retention is your whole game.

Your method: LOC with rich cream last. Seal immediately after moisturizing. Regular light proteins to fill cuticle gaps. Always moisturize within 48 hours after protein.

Test at home — two methods

The water glass test: place a clean, product-free shed hair in room-temperature water. Floats for several minutes = low porosity. Sinks gradually = medium. Sinks fast = high.

The spray bottle test: mist a small dry section. Water beads and slides off = low. Absorbed steadily = medium. Disappears almost instantly = high.

Use these as approximate indicators — not precise measurements. How your hair behaves over several wash weeks tells you far more than any single test.

WATCH FOR — POROSITY CHANGES

Color, heat, and chemical treatments raise porosity. A woman who was low porosity before coloring may find herself at high porosity. Retest every 3 to 6 months, especially after a major routine or treatment change.

modules.title: Density — how much hair you actually have
modules.html:
Density — how much hair you actually have

"I kept buying products for 'thick hair' when my problem was never the strand — it was how few of them I had. Density was the missing word." Density and thickness get confused constantly, and the mix-up sends women to all the wrong products. Density is how many strands grow on your scalp — how full your head of hair is. Thickness (next chapter) is how wide each individual strand is. You can have very fine strands packed densely, or thick strands spread sparsely. Knowing your density tells you how much product to reach for, which styles will flatter, and why your hair behaves the way it does.

The 5-second density test

Look in a mirror in good light and gently part your dry, unstretched hair down the middle. How much scalp do you see?

Low Scalp shows easily along the part Medium Some scalp shows, not a lot High Scalp is hard to see at all

FIGURE 2 — READING DENSITY AT THE PART

A second check: a loose ponytail under 5 cm (2 in) around = low · 5–10 cm = medium · over 10 cm = high.

Low density

CHARACTERISTICS

Fuller-looking when dry and shrunken, flatter when wet. Scalp visible at the part. Styles can look thin at the ends.

CHALLENGES

Heavy butters and oils flatten and separate the hair, making it look thinner. Buildup shows fast. Over-manipulation reveals scalp.

BEST PRODUCTS

Lightweight leave-ins, whipped (not greasy) creams, foaming or mousse stylers, volumizing flaxseed gel. Less is more.

BEST STYLING

Fluffy twist-outs and braid-outs for fullness, rod sets, curlformers. Keep ends blunt and well-sealed so they don't look wispy.

Medium density

CHARACTERISTICS

The most adaptable. Holds a wide range of styles, balances volume and definition without much fuss.

CHALLENGES

Few — your main job is consistency. It's easy to coast and skip deep conditioning because nothing looks "wrong" yet.

BEST PRODUCTS

Most creams and gels work. Match richness to your porosity, not your density. Adjust by season.

BEST STYLING

Nearly anything: wash-and-gos, twist- outs, protective styles. Let porosity and pattern lead your choices.

High density

CHARACTERISTICS

Lots of hair — dramatic volume, big shrinkage, real weight when wet. Looks and feels abundant.

CHALLENGES

Product and water don't reach the inner layers easily. Detangling and drying take longer. Roots can stay damp and smell if not dried fully.

BEST PRODUCTS

Richer creams and butters, generous amounts, applied in 4–8 sections so every strand is reached.

BEST STYLING

Chunky twists, banding to stretch, low- manipulation buns. Always section to dry fully at the roots.

SCHICGIRL TIP — SECTION TO YOUR DENSITY

Low density: 2 sections is plenty. Medium: 4. High: 6–8. Sectioning isn't fussy — it's the single biggest reason some women's products "work" and others' sit on top. Every strand deserves to be touched.

COMMON MISTAKE

Treating thinning at the edges or crown as "low density." Sudden or patchy loss is a scalp and health question (see the Scalp Health chapter), not a density category. Density describes the hair you naturally grow — not hair you're losing.

modules.title: Strand thickness — the width of a single hair
modules.html:
Strand thickness — the width of a single hair

"The same protein treatment that saved my friend's hair wrecked mine. We had the same curl pattern — but completely different strands." If density is how many, thickness (or "strand width") is how wide each strand is. It's quietly one of the most important things to know, because it predicts two things that make or break a routine: how likely your hair is to break, and how it responds to protein. Two women with identical 4C coils can need opposite products simply because one has fine strands and the other coarse.

The self-assessment — find your width

Pull one clean, shed strand and roll it between your fingertips. Better still, lay it on a sheet of paper next to a strand of sewing thread.

Fine Can barely feel it · thinner than thread · often hard to see Medium Feel it slightly · about as wide as thread Coarse Distinctly feel it · thicker than thread · wiry, strong

FIGURE 3 — COMPARING A STRAND TO SEWING THREAD

Test strands from several areas — many women are fine at the temples and coarser at the crown.

What thickness changes about your routine

IF YOUR

STRANDS

ARE…

BREAKAGE RISK

PROTEIN SENSITIVITY

MOISTURE RETENTION

Fine Higher — snaps easily, especially wet. Handle gently.

Very sensitive. Too much protein = instant brittleness.

Use light protein rarely.

Loses moisture fast; weighs down easily, so use light layers more often.

Medium Moderate — forgiving and balanced.

Tolerant. A monthly light protein is usually welcome.

Holds moisture reasonably.

The easiest to balance.

Coarse Lower per strand — strong, but rigid and prone to dryness.

Often needs protein to feel its best. Can take richer treatments.

Retains moisture longer but is harder to penetrate — heat helps a lot.

THE HIDDEN RULE OF TYPE 4 BREAKAGE

Fine + high porosity is the most fragile combination there is — gentle handling and moisture-first care matter more than any product. Coarse + low porosity is the most stubborn — it needs heat and patience to absorb. Know your pair, and you'll stop blaming yourself for "doing everything right."

SCHICGIRL TIP — READ WIDTH AND POROSITY TOGETHER

Thickness tells you how much protein your hair can handle; porosity tells you how often. Fine low- porosity hair almost never needs protein. Coarse high-porosity hair often loves it. You'll combine both in your profile worksheet at the back.

COMMON MISTAKE

Fine-haired women assuming "fine = needs strengthening protein." The opposite is usually true: fine strands are protein-sensitive and break more when over-proteined. Strengthen fine hair with gentle handling and moisture first, protein only in tiny, occasional doses.

modules.title: Protein vs. moisture — finding your balance
modules.html:
Protein vs. moisture — finding your balance

"For a year I added more and more moisture to hair that was begging for the opposite. The day I learned to tell the two apart, the breakage stopped." Healthy hair is a partnership between two things: moisture, which keeps strands soft and flexible, and protein, which keeps them strong and structured. Picture a fishing net — protein is the rope, moisture is the water flowing through it. Too much water and no rope, the net goes limp; all rope and no water, it goes stiff and cracks. Almost every "mystery" breakage is really one of these two tipping out of balance. The good news: your hair tells you which, clearly, once you know the signs.

The two ways hair goes out of balance

Moisture overload

TOO SOFT (HYGRAL FATIGUE)

Hair feels mushy, limp, overly soft, almost gummy when wet. Stretches like chewing gum and doesn't spring back. Won't hold a style, no definition, snaps with no resistance.

Common after weeks of co-washing and creaming with no protein.

Protein overload

TOO HARD (PROTEIN BUILDUP)

Hair feels rough, stiff, straw-like, hard. Looks dull, tangles, snaps cleanly with a "crisp" break. Often follows too many protein treatments, or rich protein products on fine/low-porosity hair.

The decision tree — diagnose in 10 seconds

START HERE · THE WET STRETCH TEST

Stretch one wet strand gently and watch what it does ▾ ▾ It over-stretches & stays limp → needs

PROTEIN

YOU'LL ALSO NOTICE

Mushy, gummy, no bounce, won't hold a twist- out, feels "too soft."

DO THIS

One light protein treatment (rice water, hydrolyzed protein, or the fenugreek mask). Then a moisturizing deep condition within 48h. Pause heavy creams briefly.

It snaps fast & feels stiff → needs MOISTURE

YOU'LL ALSO NOTICE

Hard, rough, straw-like, dull, brittle, cracks when bent.

DO THIS

Stop all protein. A rich, protein-free moisturizing deep condition with heat, weekly. Clarify if products contain protein. Add humectants and seal.

FIGURE 4 — PROTEIN / MOISTURE DECISION TREE

Balanced hair stretches a little and springs right back — soft and strong. That's your target.

Best ingredients for each side

GOAL

REACH FOR

HOW OFTEN

More moisture Water, aloe, glycerin, honey, panthenol; shea, avocado, mango butters; protein-free deep conditioners Weekly deep condition; daily/refresh as needed More protein Rice water (light), hydrolyzed wheat/silk/keratin, fenugreek, banana & yogurt masks (mild)

Every 4–6 weeks (coarse/high- po) · rarely (fine/low-po)

SCHICGIRL TIP — ALWAYS CLOSE PROTEIN WITH MOISTURE

Protein temporarily hardens the cuticle. Never end a wash day on protein — always follow it with a moisturizing step within 48 hours. Think of it as tightening the rope, then refilling the water.

COMMON MISTAKE

Treating every breakage as "needs protein." More often, Type 4 breakage is dryness. When unsure, default to moisture first and re-test — moisture overload is far easier to correct than the brittle damage protein overload causes.

modules.title: Scalp health — where every strand begins
modules.html:
Scalp health — where every strand begins

"I spent all my energy on my ends and ignored my scalp. But healthy hair is grown, not bought — and it's grown in the soil at the root." Your scalp is skin — living, breathing skin that happens to grow hair. Think of it as the soil of a garden: you can water the leaves all you like, but a strong plant comes from healthy ground. A flaking, tight, inflamed, or clogged scalp slows growth and weakens new strands before they ever reach your ends. Most Type 4 routines fuss endlessly over length and forget the foundation. Let's fix that.

Know what you're dealing with

SIGN

WHAT IT USUALLY IS

FIRST MOVE

Large, oily yellowish flakes + itch Dandruff (often a yeast imbalance)

Anti-dandruff or zinc/tea-tree wash; cleanse more regularly Small, dry white flakes, tight feeling Dry scalp (often over-washing or hard water)

Gentler cleanse, lukewarm water, light scalp oil after Coated, grey, "can't get clean" Product buildup Clarify or gently exfoliate the scalp Itching with no flakes Dryness, buildup, or reaction to a product Clarify, simplify products, check for irritants Redness, soreness, bumps, heat Inflammation (tension, irritation, infection)

Loosen styles, soothe; see a dermatologist if it lingers

DRY SCALP VS. DANDRUFF — THE QUICK TELL

Dry scalp flakes are small, white, and powdery, and your skin feels tight elsewhere too. Dandruff flakes are larger, yellowish, slightly oily, and come with more itch. Treating one as the other is why so many "anti-dandruff" routines fail — match the cause.

How often should you cleanse?

DRY / LOW OIL

Every 7–10 days. Over-washing strips an already-dry scalp and triggers more flaking.

BALANCED

Every 5–7 days. The comfortable middle for most Type 4 scalps.

OILY / ACTIVE / SWEAT

Every 3–5 days, or a focused scalp-only cleanse between full washes.

UNDER BRAIDS/TWISTS

Every 1–2 weeks with a diluted cleanser or applicator bottle — buildup under styles is the #1 cause of scalp trouble.

The 5-minute scalp massage protocol

A regular massage does two real things: it lifts buildup and, by increasing blood flow, brings nutrients to the follicle. Do it 3–4× a week, on dry or oiled hair, never with nails. Warm a little oil (jojoba, or a lightweight blend with rosemary or peppermint) between your palms — a few drops is plenty.

Use the pads of your fingers, never nails. Place them flat against the scalp.

Move the scalp, not your fingers. Make small, firm circles so the skin shifts over the skull — that's where the benefit is.

Work in sections — hairline, crown, nape, sides — about 60 seconds each.

Finish calm. A few slow strokes. Best done at night, or before a wash to loosen buildup.

SCHICGIRL TIP — EDGES ARE SCALP TOO

Your delicate edges are governed by the same skin and the same tension rules as the rest of your scalp. Massage them gently, never style them tight, and treat any soreness there as an early warning — not something to push through.

WHEN TO SEE A PROFESSIONAL

Persistent flaking that won't clear, sudden or patchy shedding, painful bumps, widening parts, or bald patches deserve a dermatologist or trichologist. This is care, not failure — some scalp conditions are medical and respond beautifully to the right treatment.

modules.title: Reading your hair's signals
modules.html:
Reading your hair's signals

"My hair had been talking to me for years. I just didn't know how to listen." Before you even touch a product, your hair is already telling you exactly what it needs — if you know how to read its signals. This skill, once developed, is more valuable than any online recommendation.

Signs of active dehydration

SIGNAL

WHAT IT MEANS

PRIORITY ACTION

Rough, dull texture Cuticle surface raised, water loss Moisturizing deep condition + sealing Clean breakage, no stretch Lack of water or protein overload Wet elasticity test first Shrinkage that sticks and tangles Severe dryness Hydration + detangle on slip Brittle, split ends Oldest part of hair, lacking protection Seal ends + protective style Scalp feels tight Internal dehydration or buildup Internal hydration + clarifying Hard, stiff hair Protein overload Moisturizing deep condition alone, no protein

Dehydration or protein overload?

These two problems look similar but require opposite solutions. The wet strand test tells them apart with precision:

LACK OF MOISTURE

Dry to the touch, breaks without stretching, dull and frizzy look, gets even drier after applying products. Solution: water first, moisturizing deep condition with heat, LOC/LCO method on soaking wet hair.

PROTEIN OVERLOAD

Stiff, hard, "crunchy" to the touch, snaps cleanly even after moisturizing. Often happens after a keratin treatment, rice water, or fortifying product. Solution: pause protein, moisturizing deep condition alone with heat for 45 min.

PRACTICAL RULE — THE MONTHLY PHOTO JOURNAL

Photograph your hair once a month, in the same light, from the same side, in the same state (washed and air-dried, with no product or with your usual routine). Photos reveal the progress your daily mirror hides — and the regressions before they set in too deeply.

modules.title: Diagnostic quiz — find your starting point
modules.html:
Diagnostic quiz — find your starting point

Answer honestly — add up your points — find your starting profile.

1. When you spray water on dry hair, it…

(1 pt)

Beads up and sits on the surface a long time (2 pts)

Absorbs steadily within a few seconds (3 pts)

Disappears almost instantly 2. Your hair takes to dry after washing…

(1 pt)

Forever — often more than a day (2 pts)

A normal amount of time, a few hours (3 pts)

Very fast, under an hour 3. Products tend to…

(1 pt)

Sit on the surface or pill up (2 pts)

Behave well and absorb (3 pts)

Disappear, hair still feels thirsty 4. A shed hair dropped in a glass of water…

(1 pt)

Floats after several minutes (2 pts)

Sinks slowly over 2–3 minutes (3 pts)

Sinks quickly, under a minute 5. Heat during a deep condition makes…

(1 pt)

A huge difference, essential (2 pts)

A small to moderate difference (3 pts)

Little to no noticeable difference My total: / 15 5–8 points → Low porosity Tightly closed cuticles. LCO method. Use heat during deep conditioning. Clarify regularly to avoid buildup. Light, humectant-rich products.

9–11 points → Medium porosity Balanced cuticles. LOC or LCO based on feel. Maintain weekly deep conditioning. Adjust for the season — that's your main variable.

12–15 points → High porosity Raised cuticles. LOC method. Seal fast and rich after every moisturizing session. Light proteins every 4–6 weeks (never alone — always followed by moisture within 48h).

PART TWO

modules.title: The structure of a complete routine
modules.html:
The structure of a complete routine

"Before, my routine was a pile of gestures. Since I built a system, my hair remembers week after week." A hair routine isn't a product list — it's a rhythm organized into four distinct moments: wash day, the mid-week refresh, daily nighttime care, and a monthly reset. Each moment has a precise role.

The Schicgirl hydration pyramid

Picture your routine as a pyramid. When something isn't working, don't start at the top — start at the base. The widest layer is always the most important.

1 · The absolute foundation Water The only true moisturizer — saturate first, always, before anything else Weekly deep condition Restores moisture and elasticity deep in the fiber — non-negotiable LOC or LCO layering The right order for your porosity, on soaking wet hair Sealing and retention Keeps water in for days, not hours 5 · The peak Satin nighttime protection Every night, no exceptions Rests on · Internal health — water intake, nutrients, restorative sleep

Your week at a glance

WASH DAY

Pre-poo → cleanse → detangle on slip → deep condition with heat → LOC/LCO on soaking wet hair → style. Once a week.

MID-WEEK

Mist water or diluted leave-in over all sections, seal dry zones with a light oil.

2 to 3 times a week.

EVERY NIGHT

Satin bonnet or pillowcase, hair loosely gathered (pineapple, light twists). 30 seconds. Irreplaceable.

EVERY MONTH

Clarify to remove buildup. Retest porosity if there's been a notable change.

Photograph progress. Evaluate and adjust.

KEY TAKEAWAY

A simple rhythm held for 90 days beats a perfect routine abandoned in two weeks.

Consistency is the active ingredient no bottle contains.

modules.title: LOC and LCO methods — the right order for you
modules.html:
LOC and LCO methods — the right order for you

"I'd been applying my products in the wrong order for years. When I learned the right sequence, my hair changed within weeks." LOC and LCO are two layering orders — not interchangeable. Each suits a specific porosity. Matching them correctly is one of the most transformative changes a Type 4 woman can make to her routine.

LOC — High Porosity

LIQUID → OIL → CREAM

Open cuticles lose water fast. Oil comes early to slow evaporation while the cuticle is still open. A rich cream seals last and forms the final, most protective barrier.

LCO — Low Porosity

LIQUID → CREAM → OIL

Tightly closed cuticles absorb slowly.

Cream comes second — while the cuticle is still open from water. A light oil finishes as a fine seal that doesn't weigh hair down.

Building your layers by profile

LAYER

LOW POROSITY (LCO)

HIGH POROSITY (LOC)

L · Liquid Water, rose water, very light water-based leave-in Creamy leave-in, water base with humectants O · Oil Light oil last: jojoba, argan, grapeseed Richer oil early: avocado, castor, sesame

C ·

Cream Light to moderate cream as second layer Rich cream or butter as final sealing layer

The golden rule of application

Always apply on soaking wet hair — not damp, not lightly misted. Soaking wet. The moisture already present in the fiber is what you're sealing in. Start immediately after rinsing your deep conditioner, section by section, working from ends up to roots.

COMMON MISTAKES TO AVOID

Applying on barely damp hair · using too much product at once (emulsify in your palms first) · skipping the oil/sealing layer · copying someone else's exact amounts without adjusting for your density · touching up repeatedly after drying — every touch lifts cuticles.

modules.title: Choosing products for your profile
modules.html:
Choosing products for your profile

"I stopped looking for the miracle product and started reading labels. That's where everything changed." A few well-chosen ingredients, used correctly and consistently, outperform twenty trendy products used at random. Here's how to choose for your profile — and how to read any label in 10 seconds.

Complete selection guide by profile

PROFILE

IDEAL LEAVE-IN

IDEAL OIL

CREAM/BUTTER

ORDER

4A · Low Light, humectants up top Jojoba, argan Light

LCO

4A · High Creamy, light proteins Avocado, sesame Moderate to rich

LOC

4B · Low Humectant + slightly creamy Jojoba, castor (diluted)

Light to moderate

LCO

4B · High Emollient-rich Castor, avocado Rich + shea

LOC

4C · Low Richest in your lineup Jojoba or light coconut Shea or mango

LCO

4C · High The richest available Castor or avocado Thick shea, last

LOC

The first-five-ingredients rule

The first five ingredients make up 80 to 90% of the bottle. If the first one isn't water (aqua) for a moisturizing leave-in, it won't do what you expect. If a heavy occlusive sits in position two, it will lock in dryness rather than moisture.

START MINIMAL

Begin with just four products: a gentle shampoo, a deep conditioner, a water-based leave-in, and a sealing oil. Master these four over 6 weeks before adding anything else. Product confusion is one of the leading causes of a routine that doesn't work.

modules.title: Wash day, step by step
modules.html:
Wash day, step by step

"Wash day used to be a chore I dreaded. Since I built a clear protocol, it's become my favorite moment of the week."

1 · Pre-poo (optional but highly recommended)

Before shampooing, apply a vegetable oil (jojoba, avocado, olive) along the full length, focusing on the ends. Leave for 20 to 30 minutes. This protects the fiber from shampoo, makes post-wash detangling significantly easier, and preserves existing natural moisture.

2 · Cleansing

Wash with a shampoo free of harsh sulfates, focusing on the scalp — not the lengths. Rinse water alone will cleanse the lengths. If your hair accumulates buildup, add a clarifying shampoo once a month before your regular routine to start fresh on a clean slate.

3 · Detangling — the non-negotiable rules

Always on wet hair, with slip (conditioner, deep conditioner, or leave-in). Ends to roots — never the reverse. Fingers first on all sections. Wide-tooth comb only if needed. Never pull. Detangle section by section, holding the section near the base to limit tension on the fiber.

4 · Deep conditioning — your most important moment

Apply generously from ends to roots. Cover with a plastic cap. Add heat for 20 to 45 minutes based on your porosity — heating cap, hooded dryer, or the greenhouse method (body heat under the cap). Rinse with cool water to help the cuticle close.

5 · Layering — on soaking wet hair

Immediately after rinsing, section by section. Don't let hair dry before applying. Seal the ends last with a bit more product. Follow your LOC or LCO order based on your porosity.

6 · Styling — less is more

Twist-out, braid-out, wash-and-go, protective style — choose based on available time and your hair's condition. The golden rule: don't over-manipulate once products are applied. Every extra touch lifts cuticles and breaks the seal you just built.

QUICK WIN — PREP THE NIGHT BEFORE

Lay out all your products, your plastic cap, your comb, and your microfiber towel the night before wash day. Removing that small logistical friction often makes the difference between doing it and pushing it to next week.

modules.title: Between-wash maintenance
modules.html:
Between-wash maintenance

"The best results don't come from wash day. They come from what you do the days after."

The mid-week refresh (2–3×)

Mist water or a diluted leave-in over all sections. Seal with a bit of light oil on the driest zones. If hair is very dry, redo a light LOC/LCO on those sections only. Don't saturate — a light mist is enough. The goal is to revive, not start over.

The greenhouse method — the transformative night treatment

For very dry hair: in the evening, mist lightly, apply a light oil, cover with a plastic cap, then a satin bonnet on top. Body heat drives moisture in while you sleep. You wake up with noticeably softer hair. Use 1 to 2 times a week.

Mornings — a quick check-in, not a rebuild

If hair is still supple, a light refresh is enough. If dry, mist lightly and apply a thin layer of cream or oil. Always protect your ends — tuck them in if possible. Only restyle if absolutely necessary.

KEY TAKEAWAY

Women whose hair thrives don't spend hours on it every day. They have precise micro- habits they do consistently. Consistency beats intensity, every time.

modules.title: Nighttime protection
modules.html:
Nighttime protection

"A cotton pillowcase was absorbing every night the moisture I'd spent hours building. A satin bonnet changed everything in a single night."

Why nighttime matters as much as wash day

A cotton pillowcase absorbs a meaningful amount of your hair's moisture — every night, for 7 to 8 hours. Cotton is hygroscopic: it absorbs water. Silk and satin don't. They also glide, reducing the friction that lifts cuticles, creates tangles, and causes breakage.

In 30 seconds each night, you protect 8 hours of hair work. It's the best return on investment in your entire routine.

YOUR PROTECTION OPTIONS

Satin bonnet: the most complete solution.

Covers all hair, protects from friction in every sleep position.

Silk or satin pillowcase: useful if you dislike sleeping with a bonnet. Replace your current pillowcase — a lifetime investment.

Satin scarf: practical for wrapping hair before adding a bonnet on top.

HOW TO GATHER HAIR FOR BED

The pineapple: all hair gathered at the crown in a very loose scrunchie. Preserves defined coils.

Overnight twists: 4 to 8 loose twists before the bonnet. Preserves shape, reduces next- morning tangles.

Loose braids: one or two braids are enough for the night. Easier to undo, fewer marks.

KEY TAKEAWAY

A satin bonnet is the least expensive product in your hair routine — and the one with the best return on investment. Wear it every night, no exceptions, even if you do nothing else that evening.

modules.title: Deep conditioning — your most powerful tool
modules.html:
Deep conditioning — your most powerful tool

"If you adopt only one practice from this guide, make it the weekly deep condition. It's irreplaceable."

Moisturizing or protein — how to choose

Moisturizing treatment

WEEKLY OR BIWEEKLY

Restores water and suppleness in the fiber.

Water base, humectants, emollients.

Contains little to no protein. This is your default maintenance treatment.

Protein treatment

EVERY 4 TO 6 WEEKS

Strengthens the fiber by filling damaged areas. Absolute rule: always followed by a moisturizing treatment within 48 hours.

Otherwise hair becomes stiff and brittle.

Heat — the essential amplifier

Gentle heat opens the cuticle so active ingredients can penetrate. Essential for low porosity, where a deep condition without heat has little effect. Options: heating cap, hooded dryer, or the greenhouse method (plastic cap + body heat) for 30 to 45 minutes.

The five-step protocol

① Apply to clean, wet hair, from ends to roots. ② Cover with a plastic cap. ③ Add heat based on your porosity — more heat for low porosity. ④ Leave on for 20 to 45 minutes. ⑤ Rinse with cool water to help the cuticle close. Then apply your LOC/LCO immediately on still-soaking hair.

QUICK WIN — NO TIME?

A 20-minute deep condition under a cap still changes the outcome of everything applied after it. Duration can vary. Weekly consistency shouldn't.

modules.title: Sealing and retention — keeping moisture in
modules.html:
Sealing and retention — keeping moisture in

"My hair was moisturized in the morning and dry by afternoon. It wasn't a moisture problem — it was a retention problem." Moisturizing is only half the work. Retention keeps water in the fiber for days rather than hours. These techniques make the difference between hydration that lasts 24 hours and hydration that lasts 3 to 5 days.

The best sealants for Type 4

Richer oils and butters form the best occlusive barriers for Type 4 hair: castor oil, avocado oil, sesame oil, shea butter. After your LOC/LCO, seal the ends a bit more — they dry out first and deserve extra attention.

The overnight greenhouse method — maximum retention technique

Mist hair, apply a light oil, cover with a plastic cap, then a satin bonnet. Body heat drives moisture in while you sleep. Use 1 to 2 times a week for very dry hair or during a recovery period.

Reducing daily evaporation

Limit direct heat and air conditioning on the hair. Cover up in dry or cold wind. Keep ends in protective styles that shield them from dry air. These simple habits significantly extend hydration between wash days.

KEY TAKEAWAY

Moisturizing without retaining is filling a sieve. Always seal, protect the ends, limit evaporation. What you keep matters more than what you apply.

PART THREE

modules.title: Humectants, emollients, occlusives
modules.html:
Humectants, emollients, occlusives

"Once I understood these three families, I stopped buying at random. Every product had a precise role."

FAMILY

ROLE

EXAMPLES

WHEN TO USE IT

TRAP TO AVOID

Humectants Attract and hold water in the fiber Glycerin, aloe vera, honey, panthenol, propylene glycol First or second layer, on wet hair Glycerin in very dry air — it pulls water out of the hair Emollients Soften the cuticle, nourish the fiber Shea, avocado, jojoba, mango, argan Mid-sequence, after the liquid layer Heavy butters as first layer on low porosity Occlusives Form a barrier that slows evaporation Castor oil, petrolatum, beeswax, lanolin Always last layer, never first Using them alone on dry hair — they seal in dryness

The dew point — glycerin's climate rule

Glycerin is a powerful humectant: it pulls water from the air into the fiber. In humid air (dew point above 60°F), it's your ally. In very dry air (dew point below 40°F), it can pull moisture out of your hair instead. Check your weather app's "details" — dew point is almost always listed there. Adjust your humectants accordingly, exactly the way you choose a coat based on temperature.

UNIVERSAL LABELING RULE

The first five ingredients make up 80–90% of the product. If your moisturizing leave-in doesn't start with water (aqua), it's a treatment or mask — not a daily moisturizer. If a heavy occlusive sits in position two, it will lock in what's already there, not add hydration.

modules.title: Star ingredients for Type 4
modules.html:
Star ingredients for Type 4

Eight ingredients to know by heart — their precise role, how to use them, and what to watch for. Aloe Vera

HUMECTANT · MATCHING PH

IDEAL FOR

All porosities. Particularly soothing for irritated or sensitive scalps. Excellent as a daily spray.

HOW TO USE

In leave-in sprays, as the "L" layer of LOC/LCO, or fresh from the leaf mixed into homemade masks.

⚠ Always pair with a seal — some find pure aloe drying if not followed by an oil.

Vegetable Glycerin

HUMECTANT · CLIMATE-DEPENDENT

IDEAL FOR

Humid, mild climates. Daily mists and leave-ins.

Excellent moisture booster under the right conditions.

HOW TO USE

½ to 1 teaspoon per spray bottle, always diluted in water and sealed over. A little goes a long way.

⚠ In very dry or cold air, it can pull water out of your hair. Watch the dew point before using it freely. Shea Butter

EMOLLIENT · OCCLUSIVE · TYPE 4 STAPLE

IDEAL FOR

High porosity, Types 4B and 4C, very dry ends.

Rich, protective, versatile. The reference ingredient for Type 4 hair.

HOW TO USE

As the final sealing layer (LOC). In small amounts — a little goes far. Avoid as the first layer on low porosity.

⚠ Can weigh down low-porosity hair as a first layer. Use sparingly and as the final seal. Avocado Oil

PENETRATING EMOLLIENT · RICH

IDEAL FOR

High porosity and Types 4B/4C. One of the few oils that actually penetrates the fiber (not just sits on top).

HOW TO USE

As the oil layer in LOC, on the ends, or mixed into homemade masks to enrich a deep condition.

⚠ Too rich for very fine hair. Start with small amounts and observe.

Jojoba Oil

EMOLLIENT · MIMICS SEBUM

IDEAL FOR

All porosities. Mimics natural sebum. Excellent for the scalp and as a light seal in LCO.

HOW TO USE

As a scalp massage oil, as a light layer in LCO, or in homemade refresh sprays.

⚠ Technically a liquid wax, not an oil. Slightly different behavior — very stable and non-comedogenic. Panthenol (Pro-Vitamin B5)

PENETRATING HUMECTANT · STRENGTHENING

IDEAL FOR

Elasticity, suppleness, and shine. Especially useful for high porosity. Genuinely penetrates the fiber.

HOW TO USE

Look for it high on an ingredient list, or add pure panthenol to your sprays and homemade masks.

⚠ Gentle and well-tolerated. One of the rare actives that rarely overdoes it — recommended for every routine. Raw Honey

HUMECTANT · ANTIBACTERIAL

IDEAL FOR

Dry, dull hair. Homemade deep conditions.

Leaves Type 4 hair noticeably softer and more elastic.

HOW TO USE

1 to 2 tablespoons in a homemade mask or deep condition. Always dilute — pure honey is sticky and hard to rinse.

⚠ Like all humectants, use sparingly in very dry/cold air.

Castor Oil

OCCLUSIVE · POWERFUL SEALANT

IDEAL FOR

High porosity, Types 4B and 4C, intense end- sealing. Also used to stimulate edges and support growth.

HOW TO USE

Always as the final layer. Very thick — mix with a lighter oil (jojoba, argan) to make application easier.

⚠ Very viscous. Use a small amount and blend with a lighter oil to avoid a heavy effect.

modules.title: Reading a label in 10 seconds
modules.html:
Reading a label in 10 seconds

"I didn't need more products. I needed to know how to read what I already had."

POSITION

WHAT IT MEANS

WHAT TO LOOK FOR

1st ingredient Main base of the product (50– 80%)

Aqua/Water for any moisturizer. Otherwise, it's a treatment or mask.

2nd–5th Primary actives (15–40% combined)

Humectants (glycerin, aloe, panthenol) ranked high = good sign 6th–10th Secondary actives and modifiers Emollients (shea, oils) — their position indicates concentration End of list Preservatives, fragrances, trace actives Preservatives are necessary for safety. Fragrance is optional.

Ingredients to know — the quick decoder

OFTEN USEFUL, RANKED EARLY

Aqua / Water · Aloe Barbadensis · Glycerin · Panthenol · Butyrospermum Parkii (shea) · Persea Gratissima (avocado) · Simmondsia Chinensis (jojoba) · Hydrolyzed proteins · Sodium PCA

WORTH WATCHING, BASED ON YOUR

ROUTINE

Sodium Lauryl Sulfate (SLS) · Alcohol Denat. / SD Alcohol · Dimethicone (non-soluble silicone — clarify regularly) · Mineral Oil / Petrolatum · Fragrance/Parfum (if scalp is sensitive)

NO INGREDIENT IS UNIVERSALLY BAD

Silicones, sulfates, glycerin in the cold — none of them are banned. What matters is whether they fit your routine and your profile. A woman who clarifies regularly can use silicones without issue. A woman in dry air should watch her glycerin. Your context decides, not a blanket rule.

PART FOUR

modules.title: Your hair is growing. You're just losing it.
modules.html:
Your hair is growing. You're just losing it.

"I was convinced my hair 'didn't grow.' It grew the whole time — I was snapping it off at the ends as fast as it came in. Nobody ever told me those were two different problems." This may be the most freeing chapter in the book. Here is the truth almost no one tells you: your hair is almost certainly growing normally. The scalp of a healthy woman grows roughly 1–1.5 cm (about half an inch) per month — close to 15 cm (6 inches) a year — and that's true across all hair types. Growth happens at the root, underground, where products can't reach it. So if your hair seems "stuck" at the same length for years, the issue is almost never growth. It's retention — keeping the length you already grew.

Growth vs. retention — two different jobs

GROWTH (THE ROOT)

Happens at the follicle. Driven by genetics, health, nutrition, scalp circulation, hormones, sleep. You support it — you don't force it.

RETENTION (THE LENGTH)

Happens along the strand and at the ends. Driven entirely by how you handle, moisturize, protect, and seal. This is the part you control.

Type 4 hair grows at much the same rate as any other type but has the lowest retention rate — not because it's weak, but because every tight coil is a stress point where a dry, unprotected strand snaps. You can grow 15 cm a year and see almost none of it if your ends break off at the same pace. Master retention and the length appears as if by magic — it was there all along.

What 12 months can actually look like

Below: the same head of hair, growing the same 1.25 cm a month. The gold bar is what the scalp grew. The navy bar is what reached the mirror — the length you keep. The difference is retention.

Months 0–3 grew 3.7 · kept 1.3 cm Months 0–6 grew 7.5 · kept 3 cm Months 0–9 grew 11 · kept 5.5 cm Months 0–12 grew 15 · kept 9 cm What you grew What you kept (good retention)

FIGURE 9 — GROWTH YOU CAN'T CONTROL VS. LENGTH YOU CAN

With poor retention the navy bar shrinks toward zero — the scalp still grows 15 cm, but the mirror never shows it. Numbers are a healthy-average illustration; your mileage varies.

The retention checklist — where length is won or lost

PROTECT THE ENDS

Your oldest, most fragile hair. Seal them at every application; tuck them away in low styles. Most breakage happens here.

MOISTURIZE RELENTLESSLY

Dry strands snap. Supple strands bend.

Moisture is retention's foundation — a dry strand has no flexibility to survive a coil.

HANDLE GENTLY

Detangle only wet, with slip, from ends to roots. Low-manipulation styling. Friction and force are length thieves.

PROTECT AT NIGHT

Satin every night. Cotton drinks your moisture and roughens the cuticle while you sleep — eight hours of quiet damage.

SCHICGIRL TIP — DUST, DON'T CHOP

Split ends travel up the strand and cause more breakage than they prevent. You don't need big cuts — "dusting" (trimming only the frayed tips, a few millimetres, every 3–4 months) keeps ends healthy so they stop snapping mid-strand. Healthy ends retain length; ragged ones sabotage it.

COMMON MISTAKE

Chasing growth oils and "inch challenges" while ignoring the ends. No oil makes hair grow faster than your genetics allow — but neglected ends can erase a whole year of growth. Spend your energy on retention; that's where visible length is made.

modules.title: The science of protective styling
modules.html:
The science of protective styling

"Protective styles protected everyone's hair but mine — until I realized a style only protects if it's done right, kept clean, and taken down on time." A protective style does one job: it tucks your fragile ends away and reduces daily manipulation, so the length you grow survives. Done well, it's one of retention's most powerful tools. Done badly — too tight, too long, too dry — it becomes the very thing that breaks your hair and thins your edges. The style itself isn't protective; how you install, maintain, and remove it is what protects.

Good choices vs. risky ones

Genuinely protective Often damaging

Is it too tight? Listen for these

Tension is the silent cause of traction alopecia — the gradual, sometimes permanent thinning of the hairline and edges. A protective style should feel secure, never painful. Take it down or loosen it if you notice: It hurts to install, or aches for more than an hour after Little white bumps or bleeding at the roots Skin pulling at the temples, or eyes feeling "lifted" Tiny broken hairs or a tingling/burning scalp Redness, soreness, or tenderness along the hairline Loose box braids & twists (your own or low- tension extensions)

Flat twists, chunky cornrows without tension

Buns, tuck-ins, and updos with ends hidden

Two-strand twists & mini-twists on your own hair

Wigs over braided-down hair (with a barrier)

Anything that hurts, pulls, or "lifts" the eyes ✕ Tiny braids on fine edges; heavy, long extensions ✕ Glued or hard-bonded styles on natural hair ✕ Styles left in 8+ weeks (matting & locking)

✕ Tight, dry styles left unmoisturized ✕ Ends left fully exposed (not protective at all)

✕

NON-NEGOTIABLE

"Beauty is pain" is a myth that costs women their edges. A style that hurts is damaging you in real time. Ask for it looser, or take it down. Edges, once gone from tension, can be very slow — sometimes impossible — to regrow.

How long is too long?

TWISTS / BRAIDS

2–6 weeks. Past 6–8 weeks, new growth matts at the roots and removal causes serious breakage.

CORNROWS / UNDER WIG

1–2 weeks before refreshing — they tighten on new growth and trap buildup quickly.

BUNS / TUCK-INS

Daily-friendly. Rotate the placement so you're not stressing the same spot every day.

REST BETWEEN STYLES

Give your hair a few days loose, washed, and deep-conditioned before the next install.

How to moisturize a style (so it actually protects)

A protective style that dries out is just slow breakage in a pretty package. Keep it cared for: Mist 2–3× a week with a watery leave-in or a diluted refresh spray, focusing length and ends — not soaking the roots.

Seal the ends with a little oil or butter, especially on braids and twists where ends are exposed. Cleanse the scalp every 1–2 weeks with a diluted cleanser in an applicator bottle; never let buildup sit.

Cover at night with a satin scarf or bonnet — the style needs nighttime protection just like loose hair.

Take down gently — never rip. Saturate with conditioner/oil, unravel from the ends up, then deep condition.

SCHICGIRL TIP — MOISTURIZED IN, MOISTURIZED OUT

Install on freshly washed, deep-conditioned, well-moisturized hair — never on dry, dirty hair "to save it for the braids." Hair goes into a style exactly as healthy as it comes out, plus a few weeks of whatever care it received inside.

modules.title: Hard water — the hidden saboteur
modules.html:
Hard water — the hidden saboteur

"I did everything right and my hair still felt coated and dull. The problem wasn't my routine — it was pouring out of my own shower head." You can own the perfect products and still have dry, dull, coated hair — because of the water you rinse with. Hard water is water high in dissolved minerals, mostly calcium and magnesium. Every wash leaves a microscopic mineral film on your strands that builds up over time, blocking moisture from getting in and making products sit uselessly on top. For Type 4 hair, already fighting to stay hydrated, hard water can quietly undo all your effort.

Signs hard water is your problem

Hair feels coated, filmy, or "never quite clean" after washing Products stopped working even though nothing else changed Persistent dryness and dullness despite a solid routine Shampoo barely lathers; soap scum or chalky residue in your tub Increased tangling, breakage, or a tight, rough feel

QUICK CHECK

White, crusty buildup around your faucets and shower head, spots on glassware, and a shampoo that won't lather are classic hard-water tells. Cheap test strips from a hardware store confirm it in seconds.

The solutions — easiest to most thorough

SOLUTION

WHAT IT DOES

BEST FOR

Chelating shampoo Special ingredients (EDTA, gluconate, phytic/citric acid) bind and lift mineral buildup that normal clarifying can't.

The targeted fix — use every 2–4 weeks if you have hard water.

Shower filter Reduces minerals and chlorine at the source, so buildup never forms in the first place. Prevention, not rescue.

The long-term solution — a one- time install on your shower head.

Acidic rinse Diluted apple-cider-vinegar rinse (1–2 tbsp per cup of water) helps dissolve light residue and smooth the cuticle.

A gentle weekly/biweekly between-wash helper.

Final distilled rinse A last rinse with bottled distilled or filtered water leaves no mineral film behind.

Budget option, or for travelling to hard-water areas.

SCHICGIRL TIP — CHELATE, THEN FLOOD WITH MOISTURE

A chelating wash is deeply clarifying — it can leave hair squeaky and temporarily stripped. Always follow it with a rich, moisturizing deep conditioner (with heat) the same day, so you remove the minerals and refill the moisture in one session.

COMMON MISTAKE

Chelating every wash. It's powerful — overusing it strips natural oils and dries Type 4 hair out. Reserve it for every 2–4 weeks (or a filter for daily prevention), and let your regular gentle cleanser handle the rest.

modules.title: Adjusting through the seasons and dew point
modules.html:
Adjusting through the seasons and dew point

"My routine hadn't stopped working. The weather had changed. When I learned to move with the seasons, my hair stopped fighting me." Your hair lives in the air around it. The most important number you may have never looked at is the dew point — the actual amount of moisture in the air. Your humectants pull water from the air: they reward you in humidity and betray you in dryness.

DEW

POINT

THE AIR IS…

HUMECTANTS WILL…

YOUR ACTION

Below

~40°F

Very dry Pull water OUT of your hair Minimize humectants · seal hard with occlusives

~40–60°F

Comfortable Behave correctly Use humectants freely Above

~60°F

Humid Pull water IN (can cause frizz)

Humectants + a film-forming layer (flaxseed gel) to lock in style

☀ SUMMER · HEAT AND HUMIDITY

Challenges: humidity can cause swelling and frizz, sweat builds up scalp residue, UV rays weaken the fiber, salt and chlorine strip.

Adjustments: lighten your layers. Less heavy butter, more water. Watch glycerin in high humidity. Pre-wet hair before pool or ocean.

Rinse and deep condition after swimming.

❄ WINTER · COLD AND DRY AIR

Challenges: cold air and indoor heating cause massive moisture loss. Glycerin can pull moisture out of hair. Cotton coats and scarves add friction.

Adjustments: enrich your seal (shea, castor).

Reduce glycerin. Weekly greenhouse treatments. Satin-lined hats. Protective styles.

KEY TAKEAWAY

You don't need a new routine every season — move just two dials: how much humectant, and how much sealing. Read the air with your weather app, then adjust. Same logic, four times a year.

modules.title: Special situations
modules.html:
Special situations

The basics don't change — water first, always seal, protect at night. What changes is the emphasis. Here's where to place it.

Under protective styles

A protective style only protects if your hair goes in moisturized, stays maintained underneath, and comes out gently. Most women neglect maintenance during — and that's where the difference is made.

Refresh spray 2 to 3 times a week on the scalp and visible roots. Gentle massage with a light oil. Never load braids with heavy butters. Satin every night, even with a style in. Take down before week 6–8.

Transitioning hair

Transitioning means two textures on one strand, joined at a fragile line of demarcation. This junction is where breakage loves to happen. Minimize tension, no rough detangling, no heat on the line. Low-manipulation styles. Trim gradually or do the big chop when you're ready — there's no wrong timeline, only yours.

Postpartum shedding

In the months after childbirth, many women experience noticeable shedding — telogen effluvium. It's hormonal, temporary, and common. Your role is to be gentle and patient. Loose styles, hydration as priority, nourish from within (iron, water, protein). See a doctor if shedding is severe, persistent, or accompanied by bald patches.

Color-treated hair

Color lifts the cuticle and almost always raises porosity. Treat color-treated hair as high porosity, whatever it was before. LOC method, regular light proteins to fill gaps, deep condition every wash, cool rinses, and sulfate-free shampoos to preserve color.

Heat damage

True heat damage is permanent — the curl pattern is lost wherever bonds broke. No product re-curls it. Stop all heat during recovery. Hydration as priority, light proteins to support weakened areas. Cut with intention — releasing damaged ends is both grief and relief.

modules.title: Advanced troubleshooting — 6 problems, 6 solutions
modules.html:
Advanced troubleshooting — 6 problems, 6 solutions

"Most 'my hair won't moisturize' stories aren't mysteries. It's one of six things — and once you can name yours, the fix is almost always simple."

IF YOUR HAIR…

DIAGNOSIS

PRIORITY ACTION

Is dry within hours no matter what you apply You're sealing in dryness — hair wasn't wet enough to start Start over on soaking wet hair + seal immediately Used to respond to LOC and suddenly doesn't Porosity changed, buildup, or season shift Retest porosity · clarify · adjust the order Shows white flecks or a chalky film Product incompatibility or overuse (pilling)

Use less product, water first, respect the order Breaks despite constant moisturizing Over-moisturizing (hygral fatigue) or rough handling Wet strand test · light protein treatment · check technique Feels coated, heavy, won't absorb water Product buildup Clarifying shampoo + deep condition + lighten the routine Is straw-like and stiff after a treatment Protein overload Moisturizing deep condition with no protein · pause protein · 48-hour rule

The hair detective's rule

Change only one variable at a time. If you change your leave-in, your oil, and your method all at once, you'll never know what caused the problem — or what fixed it. Change one thing, observe for 4 weeks, then adjust if needed.

EMERGENCY RESET — WHEN EVERYTHING GOES WRONG

① Clarify. ② Intense moisturizing deep condition with heat. ③ LOC/LCO on soaking wet hair. ④ Satin tonight. ⑤ Observe for 2 weeks before changing anything else. This sequence resolves 80% of hair crises.

modules.title: Myth vs. reality
modules.html:
Myth vs. reality

"Half of what holds our hair back isn't a product — it's an inherited belief we never questioned."

MYTH

Oil moisturizes your hair.

REALITY

Water is the only true moisturizer. Oil seals — it retains moisture already present. Applying oil to dry hair locks the dryness in.

MYTH

Type 4 hair is inherently difficult.

REALITY

It's dry because its tight coils prevent sebum from traveling down. That's biology, not a flaw — and it's entirely manageable with the right sequence.

MYTH

Greasy hair is moisturized hair.

REALITY

Greasy means sealed — not moisturized. Hair can shine with oil and be completely dry underneath. Sealing without prior hydration locks the problem in.

MYTH

Washing less often keeps hair moisturized.

REALITY

Since water is the main moisturizer, avoiding it tends to dehydrate. Buildup also blocks absorption. Cleanse gently and regularly — care, not deprivation.

MYTH

Shrinkage means your hair isn't growing.

REALITY

Shrinkage is a sign of healthy elasticity. Hair that shrinks 70% and springs back is hydrated, healthy hair. Measure in stretched length.

MYTH

Protective styles protect on their own.

REALITY

Only if hair goes in moisturized, is maintained underneath, and comes out gently. A neglected style dries out and damages just as fast as an abandoned routine.

MYTH

More products is better.

REALITY

Five well-chosen actives outperform twenty used at random. Excess becomes buildup, which blocks hydration. Mastered simplicity always beats improvised complexity.

modules.title: Transformation stories & case studies
modules.html:
Transformation stories & case studies

Behind every healthy head of hair is a woman who once wanted to give up. These journeys show what this guide makes possible.

Aïcha

4C · LOW POROSITY · TURNING POINT AT 12 WEEKS

HER STARTING POINT

Daily oiling, never a deep condition, hair dry by noon and breaking at the nape. Convinced her hair was "just difficult."

THE TURNING POINT

Discovered she was low porosity — the oil was sealing in dryness. Switched to LCO, added a weekly deep condition with heat, clarified the buildup.

✦ Twelve weeks later: moisture lasting three days, no more breakage at the nape, and — in her own words — "I finally stopped fighting my own head." Mariam

4B · HIGH POROSITY · TURNING POINT AT 8 WEEKS

HER STARTING POINT

Misting and creaming daily, yet handfuls of breakage. Felt punished for trying so hard with no visible result.

THE TURNING POINT

The wet strand test revealed over-moisturizing (hygral fatigue). Added a light protein treatment, switched to LOC, finger-detangled on slip.

✦ Eight weeks later: elasticity restored, breakage sharply down, and a routine that finally felt like balance instead of effort.

Fatou

4A · MEDIUM POROSITY · SEASONAL ADJUSTMENT

HER STARTING POINT

A summer of soft, defined coils collapsed into dry, brittle hair every winter. She blamed her products and bought more.

THE TURNING POINT

Learned to read the dew point. Cut back her glycerin spray in the cold, sealed richer with avocado and shea, added a weekly greenhouse night.

✦ By spring: no winter slump for the first time in years — same hair, two dials adjusted.

Three journeys in depth

The stories above in detail — so you can see exactly how a diagnosis becomes a routine, and a routine becomes results.

CASE STUDY 01

The hair that drank oil and stayed dry 4C · Low porosity · Chronic dryness & nape breakage

STARTING POINT

Oiled her hair daily, never deep-conditioned, and was dry by noon with constant breakage at the nape. Heavy butters sat on top and flaked. She was convinced her 4C hair was "just difficult" and had spent months buying richer and richer products that only made it worse.

DIAGNOSIS

The water-glass and spray tests both pointed to low porosity: her tightly closed cuticles were never letting water in — so the daily oil was sealing dryness in and moisture out. Her elasticity test was poor and her scalp showed buildup.

THE SOLUTION

Clarify to remove the months of buildup. Switch from oil-first to the LCO method (cream before oil), apply on soaking-wet hair, and add a weekly moisturizing deep condition with heat to coax the cuticle open. Lighter products, sealing the nape and ends specifically, satin every night.

RESULTS AT 12 WEEKS

Moisture lasting three days instead of three hours. Nape breakage stopped. In her words: "I finally stopped fighting my own head."

LESSONS

Low-porosity hair needs heat and water first, not more oil. Oil seals whatever is underneath — including dryness. Diagnose porosity before you choose a method.

CASE STUDY 02

Doing everything right, breaking anyway 4A · High porosity · Relentless breakage

STARTING POINT

Misted and creamed her hair every single day, yet lost handfuls to breakage. She felt punished for trying so hard with nothing to show for it — the more she moisturized, the mushier and weaker her hair felt.

DIAGNOSIS

The wet-stretch test was the giveaway: strands over-stretched and stayed limp — classic moisture overload (hygral fatigue) on already high-porosity hair. Constant daily moisture with zero protein had left the cortex with no structure.

THE SOLUTION

One light protein treatment to rebuild structure, followed by a moisturizing deep condition within 48 hours. Switched to the LOC method (oil early to slow her fast evaporation, rich cream to seal), cut daily creaming back to every 2–3 days, and finger-detangled only on slip.

RESULTS AT 8 WEEKS

Elasticity restored, breakage sharply down, and a routine that finally felt like balance instead of endless effort.

LESSONS

More moisture isn't always the answer. High-porosity hair needs protein to hold structure and sealing to keep water in. When breakage persists despite moisture, test for overload before adding more.

CASE STUDY 03

Two textures, one head — surviving the transition Transitioning · Relaxed ends + natural roots · Breakage at the line of demarcation

STARTING POINT

Growing out a relaxer without the big chop. Her hair was snapping right where the new natural growth met the straight, processed ends — the fragile "line of demarcation" — and she was close to giving up and relaxing again.

DIAGNOSIS

Two textures with completely different needs on one strand, and the join point bearing all the stress. The relaxed ends were high-porosity and damaged; any tension, dryness, or rough detangling broke the strand exactly at the weakest seam.

RECOVERY PLAN

Low-manipulation, low-tension protective styles (twists and roller sets) that blended the two textures and kept the demarcation line from being handled. Weekly moisturizing deep conditioning, a gentle monthly protein for the fragile ends, detangling only wet with slip from ends up — and regular "dusting" trims to slowly remove the relaxed ends over time.

RESULTS

Breakage at the line dropped dramatically; she retained her new growth and trimmed away the last relaxed ends after several months — fully natural, with length kept.

LESSONS

Transitioning is about protecting the demarcation line: keep both textures moisturized, minimize manipulation, and trim gradually. Patience retains length; force snaps it. (See the Transitioning Hair guide for the full transitioning protocol.)

WHAT EVERY STORY SHARES

Not a miracle product, but a moment of understanding. The transformation began the day she learned to read her hair — exactly what you're doing right now.

WORKSHEETS & APPENDICES

modules.title: 6 Essential DIY Recipes
modules.html:
6 Essential DIY Recipes

Simple, affordable, and effective because they're formulated with pure ingredients. Always patch- test a new blend on a small area before full application. Follow storage guidance strictly. 1 · Daily Refresh Spray

INGREDIENTS

200 ml distilled water (or rose water)

2 tbsp pure aloe vera gel 1 tsp vegetable glycerin 1 tsp jojoba oil 5 drops lavender essential oil (optional — always dilute, always patch-test)

METHOD

Combine water and aloe in a clean spray bottle. Add glycerin and jojoba. Shake for 30 seconds. Shake before every use.

Storage: refrigerate, use within 5 days. Freeze in portions for longer storage. Discard at the first change. · Frequency: daily or between washes.

2 · Banana & Honey Deep Conditioner

INGREDIENTS

1 very ripe banana · 2 tbsp raw honey 2 tbsp avocado oil · 2 tbsp plain full-fat yogurt 1 tsp panthenol (optional)

METHOD

Blend the banana until perfectly smooth. Mix in the honey, oil, yogurt, and panthenol. Apply to clean, wet hair, cover with a cap, 30–45 min with heat. Rinse with cool water.

Storage: use immediately. · Frequency: every 1 to 2 weeks.

3 · Homemade Flaxseed Gel

INGREDIENTS

250 ml water · 2 tbsp whole flaxseeds 1 tsp aloe vera gel · 1 tsp glycerin

METHOD

Heat the water and seeds, stirring, until the mixture thickens (7–10 min). Strain immediately into a jar. Let cool, then stir in the aloe and glycerin. Shake before use.

Storage: refrigerate, use within 5–7 days. Freeze in portions. · Frequency: wash day, as a styler. 4 · Light Shea & Aloe Leave-In

INGREDIENTS

100 ml rose water · 2 tbsp aloe vera gel 1 tsp shea butter, melted · 1 tsp jojoba oil ½ tsp glycerin

METHOD

Melt the shea gently, let it cool slightly. Combine rose water, aloe, and glycerin in a bottle. Add jojoba and the liquid shea. Shake vigorously for 1 minute. Shake before every use.

Storage: refrigerate, use within a week. · Frequency: wash day and as needed.

5 · Fenugreek & Hibiscus Strengthening Mask

INGREDIENTS

2 tbsp fenugreek seeds (soaked overnight)

1 tbsp hibiscus powder · 2 tbsp avocado oil 1 tbsp plain yogurt

METHOD

Blend the drained fenugreek seeds into a smooth paste. Mix in the hibiscus powder, oil, and yogurt. Apply to clean, wet hair, cover, leave 20–30 min. Rinse thoroughly. Always follow with a moisturizing deep condition within 48 hours.

⚠ Lightly proteinaceous — use no more than once every 4 to 6 weeks. Strong scent. Thorough rinsing essential. · Frequency: monthly or based on elasticity test.

6 · Cooling Scalp Mist

INGREDIENTS

150 ml strong peppermint infusion, cooled 50 ml rose water · 5 drops tea tree oil 1 tsp jojoba oil · 1 tsp aloe vera extract

METHOD

Brew a strong infusion, let it cool completely. Mix with rose water in a spray bottle. Add oils and aloe. Shake. Spray on the scalp between washes. Patch-test 24h before first use.

Storage: refrigerate, use within 5 days. · Frequency: 2 to 3× a week on the scalp.

Product Evaluation Sheet

Stop guessing whether a product works. Track each one for two weeks, then decide with evidence — not marketing. Photocopy this page for every new bottle.

PRODUCT & TYPE

FIRST 5 INGREDIENTS

HOW MY HAIR FELT (WEEK 1–2)

REPURCHASE?

☐ Yes ☐ No ☐ Maybe ☐ Yes ☐ No ☐ Maybe ☐ Yes ☐ No ☐ Maybe ☐ Yes ☐ No ☐ Maybe ☐ Yes ☐ No ☐ Maybe

THE 4 QUESTIONS FOR ANY PRODUCT

Does it match my porosity? (Light + humectant for low · rich + sealing for high)

Are humectants/emollients in the first five ingredients — and is water near the top of a moisturizer?

Does it contain protein? (Fine/low-porosity → use sparingly · coarse/high-porosity → welcome)

After 2 weeks, is my hair more moisturized, defined, and intact — or coated, dry, or breaking?

SCHICGIRL TIP — ONE NEW PRODUCT AT A TIME

Introduce products one at a time, two weeks apart. If you swap three things at once and your hair changes, you'll never know which one did it — good or bad. Patience here saves you a shelf of expensive mistakes.

90-Day Transformation Planner

A simple rhythm held for 90 days produces results no product can replace. Use the map below to know what to focus on each fortnight, then track your wins.

PERIOD

FOCUS

WHAT YOU DO

Week 1 Diagnosis Porosity quiz. Identify your pattern. Photograph today. Fill in your profile worksheet. Elasticity test.

Week 2 Reset Clarify. Intense deep condition with heat. Begin nightly satin protection.

Start fresh on a clean slate.

Weeks 3–4 Sequence LOC or LCO at every wash on soaking wet hair. Refresh 2× mid-week.

Observe how long moisture lasts.

Weeks 5–6 Deep conditioning Weekly deep condition with heat, no exceptions. Try the greenhouse method 1× this week. Note the difference the next morning.

Weeks 7–8 Retention Track how many days moisture lasts. Adjust sealing if needed. Seal ends more generously.

Weeks 9– Adjustment Compare with your starting photos. Identify one thing to improve.

Change a single element — observe 4 weeks.

Weeks 11– Anchoring Your routine is set. Turn it into a ritual — music, tea, a calm space.

Consistency comes from enjoyment.

Plan the next 90 days.

modules.title: Cheat Sheet
modules.html:
Cheat Sheet

Everything that matters, on one page. Photocopy and tape inside your bathroom cabinet.

THE HYDRATION HIERARCHY — ALWAYS IN THIS ORDER

1. Water (the only true moisturizer) → 2. Humectant (attracts & holds water) → 3. Emollient (softens, smooths) → 4. Occlusive (seals it all in). Skip a layer and the ones after have nothing left to retain.

LOC OR LCO — WHICH IS MINE?

High porosity → LOC. Oil comes early to slow fast evaporation. Thick cream seals last.

Low porosity → LCO. Cream enters while the cuticle is open. Light oil seals last without weighing hair down.

FREQUENCY — QUICK REFERENCE

Moisturizing deep condition: weekly Light protein treatment: 4–6 weeks Clarify: 2–4 weeks Refresh: 2–3× / week Satin: every night Retest porosity: 3–6 months

IF THIS… → THEN THAT

Dry within hours Hair wasn't wet enough + missing seal → restart on soaking wet hair Coated, won't absorb Buildup → clarify + deep condition White flecks / pilling Too much product or wrong order → use less + water first Straw-like and stiff Protein overload → moisturizing treatment with no protein, pause Breaks despite moisture Elasticity test → over-moisturizing or rough handling Collapses every winter Reduce humectants + seal richer + weekly greenhouse

SCHICGIRL GLOSSARY

Humectant — draws water into the fiber (glycerin, aloe, honey, panthenol)

Emollient — softens and smooths the fiber (shea, jojoba, avocado)

Occlusive — forms a barrier that seals in water (castor, thick butters)

Porosity — how easily the cuticle lets water in and out Cuticle — outer scaled layer that holds in moisture LOC / LCO — layering order: Liquid, Oil, Cream Dew point — amount of moisture in the air; governs humectants Hygral fatigue — weakness from over-moisturizing Greenhouse method — body heat under a cap to drive in moisture Clarify — deep cleanse to remove buildup Slip — how slippery a product is, for easy detangling Pre-poo — protective treatment applied before shampoo

modules.title: Disclaimers & safety
modules.html:
Disclaimers & safety

GENERAL DISCLAIMER

This guide is provided for educational and informational purposes only. It reflects the author's experience and research on natural Type 4 hair care. Every head of hair is unique: results vary based on porosity, density, health, climate, and consistency. Nothing here guarantees specific results.

MEDICAL DISCLAIMER

The content in this guide does not constitute medical advice and does not replace professional care. Hair loss, persistent scalp irritation, flaking, or sudden shedding may have medical causes. Consult a physician or dermatologist. Consult a healthcare professional before starting any supplement.

ESSENTIAL OIL SAFETY

Essential oils are potent. Always dilute them in a carrier oil before application. Patch-test 24 hours before first use. Some are not recommended during pregnancy or for young children.
